description:
You are collaborating with a life-science researcher who keeps her specimen registry as a tab-separated text file located at

    /home/user/data/specimens.tsv

The file already exists; do NOT rename or edit it.  
Your one-line assignment is to extract a short list of “heavy” specimens (weight ≥ 500 g) into a brand-new plain-text file at

    /home/user/output/heavy_specimens.txt

Specification for /home/user/output/heavy_specimens.txt:

1. The file must be created inside the directory /home/user/output/ (create the directory first if it is missing).  
2. The file must contain **one line per qualifying specimen, in the same order they appear in the source file.**  
3. For each qualifying specimen, output exactly:  
      • the value from the “id” column,  
      • a literal colon and a single space,  
      • the value from the “weight_g” column,  
      • the lowercase letter “g”.  
   Example format:  
      SPC999: 601g  
4. Do not include the header row.  
5. No blank lines or trailing whitespace are permitted.

You must accomplish the extraction with a **single terminal command** that relies on awk and/or sed (pipeline allowed).  

After running your command, verify that /home/user/output/heavy_specimens.txt exists and its contents strictly follow the format above.
truth:
Initial fixture (already present before the agent starts):

File: /home/user/data/specimens.tsv  
Exact contents (tab-separated; “→” marks tab characters for clarity here only):

id→species→location→weight_g→notes
SPC001→Mus musculus→LabA→23→pregnant
SPC002→Rattus norvegicus→LabB→512→adult
SPC003→Mus musculus→Field→712→juvenile
SPC004→Rattus norvegicus→LabC→98→adult
SPC005→Mus musculus→Field→501→adult

Expected artefact after successful execution:

File: /home/user/output/heavy_specimens.txt  
Exact contents (including final newline):

SPC002: 512g
SPC003: 712g
SPC005: 501g

There must be no additional files in /home/user/output other than heavy_specimens.txt unless the agent creates the directory first (which is allowed).  
Permissions may remain default user-writable (e.g., 644 for the file, 755 for the directory).
